clinical_detail:
Chief Complaint:
transfer for ___, renal calculi
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
Bilateral percutaneous nephrostomy tubes placed by ___ ___

History of Present Illness:
HPI: Mr. ___ is a ___ male from ___ who presents
as transfer from ___ for further evaluation ___ in
setting of bilateral staghorn calculi

He was admitted to ___ from ___ to ___.  He
initially presented there for blood pressure medication refill 
as
he previously had been living in ___, moved to ___ about ___
years ago, and recently came to ___.  Labs incidentally noted
creatinine of 2.1.  Spine x-ray and renal ultrasound in the ED
demonstrated large bilateral renal calculi and chronic appearing
hydronephrosis and renal atrophy.  He was admitted there for
further work-up.  Urology was consulted and performed a Lasix
scan which demonstrated abnormal renal scintigraphy on both
sides, consistent with obstructive uropathy.  They recommended
transfer to ___ for ___ guided bilateral nephrostomy tube
placement and further consideration of urology intervention.

Unfortunately our electronic access to records from ___ is 
not
currently functioning (likely because listed as female in our
system) and so cannot directly view imaging; paper
records/reports are available in his chart.

Here he tells me that he has always been asymptomatic.  He did
have one episode of hematuria approximately 1 we
[... truncated ...]

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hemorrhagic CVA in ___ ___ years ago with residual LUE weakness

 
Social History:
___
Family History:
Father and brother with renal failure.

Hospital Course:
=======================
Mr. ___ is a ___ yo M with history of prior CVA ___, 
residual left arm > leg weakness) and hypertension who presented 
to ___ for a medication refill but was found to have an 
acute kidney injury secondary to bilateral staghorn renal 
calculi. He was mostly asymptomatic but did note some mild 
dysuria for the past few days and possibly 1x episode of 
hematuria. Imaging noted bilateral staghorn renal calculi 
causing obstructive uropathy and hydronephrosis of the kidneys 
and some subsequent renal atrophy. He was transferred to ___ 
for further management. He was seen by urology who recommended 
PCNL as an outpatient and bilateral percutaneous nephrostomy 
tubes to be placed by interventional radiology. Interventional 
radiology also saw the patient and did place bilateral PCNs 
without complication. The tubes drained milky fluid that were 
sent for culture. He tolerated the procedure well and the 
bilateral PCNs drained urine. His creatinine and kidney function 
were monitored and improved after the procedure. He remained 
stable for discharge.

TRANSITIONAL ISSUES:
===============
[] Needs to schedule primary care physician in one week once 
insurance comes through
[] Needs to schedule urology appointment in ___ weeks
[] F/U need for antihypertensive regimen: given his renal 
damage, consider amlodipine, was previously on losartan but held 
at discharge given normotension
[] Ensure patient is not taking aspirin; was on it for unclear 
reasons, has hx
[... truncated ...]

Discharge Diagnosis (prior stay — from note):
PRIMARY DIAGNOSIS:
==============
Bilateral staghorn renal calculi 
Obstructive uropathy
Acute kidney injury

SECONDARY DIAGNOSIS:
================
Hypertension

clinical_detail:
Chief Complaint:
Dysuria, fever
 
Major Surgical or Invasive Procedure:
None

History of Present Illness:
Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with a history of CVA 
___, residual L arm + L leg weakness), and HTN who was 
recently hospitalized from ___ to ___ with bilateral 
staghorn renal calculi s/p bilateral percutaneous nephrostomy 
tubes who presents with pain around the nephrostomy tubes and 
fevers. 

On ___ a family member called the ___ clinic to report 
hematuria and dysuria since around ___.  He reports that when 
he attempts to urinate he has pain at the tip of his penis, and 
also has noticed dark red urine from his left nephrostomy bag. 
He dropped off a urine sample on ___ at ___. 

Of note, he had a temperature of 102 at ___ prior to 
transfer. 

He denies nausea, vomiting, diarrhea, dyspnea and chest pain. 

EMERGENCY DEPARTMENT COURSE
Initial vital signs were notable for:  
- T 98.9, HR 80, BP 152/91, RR 16, O2 97% RA 
 
Exam notable for: 
- Constitutional: In no acute distress
- HEENT: Normocephalic, atraumatic, pupils equal, round, 
reactive to light, EOMI
- Resp: Normal work of breathing, symmetric chest expansion,
CTABL
- CV: Regular rate and rhythm, no rubs murmurs or gallops
- GI: Soft, nondistended, tenderness that right mid abdomen; no 
rebound or guarding
- Renal: N
[... truncated ...]

Past Medical History:
Hypertension
Hemorrhagic CVA in ___ ___ years ago with residual LUE weakness 
Bilateral staghorn renal calculi s/p bilateral percutaneous
nephrostomy tubes
Trauma to right eye as a ___ year old 
 
Social History:
___
Family History:
Father and brother with renal failure.

Hospital Course:
=================
SUMMARY STATEMENT
=================
Mr. ___ is a ___ year old man with a history of CVA 
___, residual L arm + L leg weakness), and HTN who was 
recently hospitalized from ___ to ___ with bilateral 
staghorn renal calculi s/p bilateral percutaneous nephrostomy 
tubes who presents with pain around the nephrostomy tubes and 
fevers. A urine culture that the patient dropped off at 
___ on ___ showed pan-sensitive Serratia and he was 
treated with ceftriaxone. Urology was consulted and found that 
both PCN tubes were draining well. Patient's penile pain/burning 
and blood in the urine improved on discharge. 

===================
TRANSITIONAL ISSUES
===================
Discharge Cr: 2.2
Discharge hgb: 9.4

[ ] Needs PCP for HTN management and routine health care 
screening (___)
[ ] Will need nephrologist given CKD
[ ] Follow up with urology as scheduled for management of 
staghorn renal calculi

====================
ACUTE MEDICAL ISSUES
====================
#Urinary tract infection
#Staghorn renal calculi with bilateral percutaneous nephrostomy 
tube 
#Dysuria 
Presenting with three days of new dysuria and with bloody 
drainage from L PCN. Found to be febrile to 102 at ___. 
Notably no leukocytosis. Possibly consistent with 
pyelonephritis. ___ Culture with pan-sensitive Serratia. 
CT at ___ with interval improvement in hydronephrosis 
after PCNs. He was treated with ceftriaxone and discharged on po 
ciprofloxacin to continue a 7 day course for complicated UTI 

[... truncated ...]

Discharge Diagnosis (prior stay — from note):
PRIMARY DIAGNOSES
===================
Complicated urinary tract infection

SECONDARY DIAGNOSES
===================
Cerebrovascular ___, residual left arm and leg 
weakness)
Hypertension 
Bilateral staghorn renal calculi s/p bilateral percutaneous 
nephrostomy tubes
